text.title: Database Selection Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every database-selection application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Relational (PostgreSQL, MySQL) for ACID transactions and relational integrity. NewSQL (CockroachDB, TiDB, Spanner, YugabyteDB) for horizontally scaled SQL with strong consistency. Document (MongoDB, DynamoDB, Couchbase) for nested or evolving schema. Key-Value (Redis, Memcached, Valkey) for cache, sessions, leaderboards. Time-Series (TimescaleDB, InfluxDB, ClickHouse, QuestDB) for metrics, telemetry, append-heavy workloads. Graph (Neo4j, Neptune, ArangoDB) for many-hop relationship queries. Vector (Qdrant, Weaviate, Pinecone, pgvector) for embedding similarity search. Search (Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Meilisearch, Typesense) for full-text and faceted retrieval.
rule.rationale: Each paradigm is optimized for one access pattern. Forcing a paradigm onto the wrong workload causes 10x cost or 10x latency.

rule.statement: CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) — finance, ledgers, inventory. AP (Availability + Partition Tolerance) — social feeds, streaming, telemetry. CA (Consistency + Availability) — single-region OLTP where partitions are rare. PACELC: when no Partition, choose PC (latency cost for consistency, e.g. Spanner) or PA (lower latency, eventual consistency, e.g. Cassandra/DynamoDB default).
rule.rationale: CAP forces a choice during a network partition; PACELC forces a choice during normal operation. Both shape the user-visible failure mode.

rule.statement: Selection MUST score ≥3 candidates across 4 criteria (access fit, consistency, scale, ops fit) on a 1-5 scale. Selecting without scoring is blocked.
rule.rationale: Scoring forces the picker to articulate trade-offs and surfaces hidden dimensions of the choice.

rule.statement: Top-2 candidates MUST be subjected to a benchmark spike against realistic data + the 3 hottest queries before final selection.
rule.rationale: Vendor benchmarks are misleading at production scale; without a spike the selection rests on marketing materials.

rule.statement: ADR MUST include a rollback_path block with engineering_weeks + dollars_contract_exit + customer impact.
rule.rationale: DB rollback is one of the highest-cost reversal scenarios; estimating it forces honest sizing of the choice.

rule.statement: DB selection ADR MUST be tagged with adr-reversibility-tagging; default expectation is partial_two_way or higher.
rule.rationale: Without reversibility tagging the selection escapes the cool-off / founder-signoff gates DB choices deserve.

rule.statement: Postgres (or any 'house default') MUST NOT be selected without scoring at least 2 alternatives. Familiarity bias is the leading cause of poor DB fit.
rule.rationale: Forcing alternatives even when the house default wins documents the choice and accelerates future revisits.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
